medications.indication_rationale: Settles the localised inflammation of contact dermatitis; source used betamethasone valerate 0.1% BD for up to 7 days to affected skin.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to affected skin twice daily for up to 7 days (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: May reduce itch as an adjunct; source used loratadine 10mg daily. Does not treat the dermatitis itself.

medications.drug_name: antivirals (treating as zoster) / oral antibiotics without infection
medications.indication_rationale: No zoster (non-dermatomal) and no secondary infection — antivirals/antibiotics are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid the nickel trigger — stop wearing the buckle or coat its back with clear nail polish / a barrier; rinse the area with lukewarm water (no scrubbing/soap) and do not pop intact blisters.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the allergen is curative and prevents recurrence; intact blister roofs protect healing skin.

patient_education_points.point: This is allergic contact dermatitis — your skin reacted to the metal (likely nickel) in the belt buckle, which is why the rash is the exact shape of the buckle. It settles with a steroid cream and, importantly, avoiding the metal.

Stop the buckle touching your skin (or paint its back with clear nail polish as a barrier), rinse gently with water only, and don't pop the blisters. The steroid cream settles the itch and redness over about a week.
See a doctor / go to ED if the redness spreads well beyond the buckle area and becomes painful, oozes thick yellow pus, or you develop a fever — that suggests a skin infection.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution; patch testing referral if recurrent/uncertain allergen.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading painful redness, pus or fever — GP/ED.

must_include_items: Identify allergic contact dermatitis and the nickel trigger; advise allergen avoidance/barrier
acceptable_alternatives: Any suitable-potency topical steroid; antihistamine as an itch adjunct; patch testing referral if recurrent
errors_of_commission: Advising blister popping / vigorous soap scrubbing
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires diagnosing contact dermatitis with allergen avoidance, a topical corticosteroid, and a secondary-infection safety-net.
